question: B-2. A particle moving on a straight line starts from rest and attains a velocity \( v_{0} \) after traversing a distance \( s_{0} \). If the motion is such that the acceleration was never increasing, find the maximum time for the traverse.
solution:
B-2 We take \( v_{0} \) as positive (see Comment) and consider the graph of \( v \) as a function of \( t \) (see Figure 1). From the given data we know that the curve starts at the origin and is concave downward since the acceleration \( a=d v / d t \) does not increase.

Fig. 1

Let \( t_{0} \) be the time of the traverse. Then \( v\left(t_{0}\right)=v_{0} \). The distance \( s_{0} \) is represented by the area bounded by the curve \( v=v(t) \), the \( t \)-axis, and the line \( t=t_{0} \). The area of the right triangle with vertices at \( (0,0),\left(t_{0}, 0\right) \) and \( \left(t_{0}, v_{0}\right) \) has area less than or equal to \( s \). Thus \( \frac{1}{2} v_{0} t_{0} \leqq s_{0} \) or
\[
t_{0} \leqq \frac{2 s_{0}}{v_{0}} .
\]

Equality is possible and gives the maximum value of \( t_{0} \) (for given \( s_{0} \) and \( v_{0} \) ) when the graph of \( v(t) \) is the straight line \( v(t)=\left(v_{0} / t_{0}\right) t=\left(v_{0}^{2} / 2 s_{0}\right) t \).

Comment: If \( v_{0} \) is zero or negative, there is no maximum time \( t_{0} \) for the traverse. In the case \( v_{0}=0 \) the equation of motion
\[
S=s_{0}\left[3\left(t / t_{0}\right)^{2}-3\left(t / t_{0}\right)^{3}\right], \quad 0 \leqq t \leqq t_{0}
\]
satisfies the conditions of the problem for any \( t_{0}>0 \).

descriptive_long.question: B-2. A particle moving on a straight line starts from rest and attains a velocity \( initveloc \) after traversing a distance \( initdispl \). If the motion is such that the acceleration was never increasing, find the maximum time for the traverse.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-2 We take \( initveloc \) as positive (see Comment) and consider the graph of \( velocity \) as a function of \( timevar \) (see Figure 1). From the given data we know that the curve starts at the origin and is concave downward since the acceleration \( accelr = d\,velocity / d\,timevar \) does not increase.

Fig. 1

Let \( traversetime \) be the time of the traverse. Then \( velocity\!\left(traversetime\right)=initveloc \). The distance \( initdispl \) is represented by the area bounded by the curve \( velocity = velocity(timevar) \), the \( timevar \)-axis, and the line \( timevar = traversetime \). The area of the right triangle with vertices at \( (0,0),\,(traversetime,0) \) and \( (traversetime,\,initveloc) \) has area less than or equal to \( displlow \). Thus \( \frac{1}{2}\, initveloc\, traversetime \leqq initdispl \) or
\[
traversetime \leqq \frac{2\, initdispl}{initveloc} .
\]

Equality is possible and gives the maximum value of \( traversetime \) (for given \( initdispl \) and \( initveloc \) ) when the graph of \( velocity(timevar) \) is the straight line \( velocity(timevar)=\left(initveloc / traversetime\right) timevar = \left(initveloc^{2} / 2\,initdispl\right) timevar \).

Comment: If \( initveloc \) is zero or negative, there is no maximum time \( traversetime \) for the traverse. In the case \( initveloc = 0 \) the equation of motion
\[
displcap = initdispl\Bigl[3\bigl(timevar / traversetime\bigr)^{2} - 3\bigl(timevar / traversetime\bigr)^{3}\Bigr], \quad 0 \leqq timevar \leqq traversetime
\]
satisfies the conditions of the problem for any \( traversetime > 0 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-2. A particle moving on a straight line starts from rest and attains a velocity \( blueberry \) after traversing a distance \( chandelier \). If the motion is such that the acceleration was never increasing, find the maximum time for the traverse.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-2 We take \( blueberry \) as positive (see Comment) and consider the graph of \( pineapple \) as a function of \( lanterns \) (see Figure 1). From the given data we know that the curve starts at the origin and is concave downward since the acceleration \( suitcase=d pineapple / d lanterns \) does not increase.

Fig. 1

Let \( harmonica \) be the time of the traverse. Then \( pineapple\left(harmonica\right)=blueberry \). The distance \( chandelier \) is represented by the area bounded by the curve \( pineapple=pineapple(lanterns) \), the \( lanterns \)-axis, and the line \( lanterns=harmonica \). The area of the right triangle with vertices at \( (0,0),\left(harmonica, 0\right) \) and \( \left(harmonica, blueberry\right) \) has area less than or equal to \( toothbrush \). Thus \( \frac{1}{2} blueberry harmonica \leqq chandelier \) or
\[
harmonica \leqq \frac{2 chandelier}{blueberry} .
\]

Equality is possible and gives the maximum value of \( harmonica \) (for given \( chandelier \) and \( blueberry \) ) when the graph of \( pineapple(lanterns) \) is the straight line \( pineapple(lanterns)=\left(blueberry / harmonica\right) lanterns=\left(blueberry^{2} / 2 chandelier\right) lanterns \).

Comment: If \( blueberry \) is zero or negative, there is no maximum time \( harmonica \) for the traverse. In the case \( blueberry=0 \) the equation of motion
\[
margarine=chandelier\left[3\left(lanterns / harmonica\right)^{2}-3\left(lanterns / harmonica\right)^{3}\right], \quad 0 \leqq lanterns \leqq harmonica
\]
satisfies the conditions of the problem for any \( harmonica>0 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-2. A particle moving on a straight line starts from rest and attains a velocity \( restfulspeed \) after traversing a distance \( restdistanceinit \). If the motion is such that the acceleration was never increasing, find the maximum time for the traverse.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-2 We take \( restfulspeed \) as positive (see Comment) and consider the graph of \( stillness \) as a function of \( timelessness \) (see Figure 1). From the given data we know that the curve starts at the origin and is concave downward since the acceleration \( stagnation=d stillness / d timelessness \) does not increase.

Fig. 1

Let \( timelesszero \) be the time of the traverse. Then \( stillness\left(timelesszero\right)=restfulspeed \). The distance \( restdistanceinit \) is represented by the area bounded by the curve \( stillness=stillness(timelessness) \), the \( timelessness \)-axis, and the line \( timelessness=timelesszero \). The area of the right triangle with vertices at \( (0,0),\left(timelesszero, 0\right) \) and \( \left(timelesszero, restfulspeed\right) \) has area less than or equal to \( standstill \). Thus \( \frac{1}{2} restfulspeed\; timelesszero \leqq restdistanceinit \) or
\[
timelesszero \leqq \frac{2\; restdistanceinit}{restfulspeed} .
\]

Equality is possible and gives the maximum value of \( timelesszero \) (for given \( restdistanceinit \) and \( restfulspeed \) ) when the graph of \( stillness(timelessness) \) is the straight line \( stillness(timelessness)=\left(restfulspeed / timelesszero\right) timelessness=\left(restfulspeed^{2} / 2\; restdistanceinit\right) timelessness \).

Comment: If \( restfulspeed \) is zero or negative, there is no maximum time \( timelesszero \) for the traverse. In the case \( restfulspeed=0 \) the equation of motion
\[
restdistance = restdistanceinit\left[3\left(timelessness / timelesszero\right)^{2}-3\left(timelessness / timelesszero\right)^{3}\right], \quad 0 \leqq timelessness \leqq timelesszero
\]
satisfies the conditions of the problem for any \( timelesszero>0 \).

garbled_string.question: B-2. A particle moving on a straight line starts from rest and attains a velocity \( asdfghjk \) after traversing a distance \( zxcvbnml \). If the motion is such that the acceleration was never increasing, find the maximum time for the traverse.
garbled_string.solution:
B-2 We take \( asdfghjk \) as positive (see Comment) and consider the graph of \( qzxwvtnp \) as a function of \( mbvcxzpo \) (see Figure 1). From the given data we know that the curve starts at the origin and is concave downward since the acceleration \( hjgrksla=d qzxwvtnp / d mbvcxzpo \) does not increase.

Fig. 1

Let \( lkjhgfds \) be the time of the traverse. Then \( qzxwvtnp\left(lkjhgfds\right)=asdfghjk \). The distance \( zxcvbnml \) is represented by the area bounded by the curve \( qzxwvtnp=qzxwvtnp(mbvcxzpo) \), the \( mbvcxzpo \)-axis, and the line \( mbvcxzpo=lkjhgfds \). The area of the right triangle with vertices at \( (0,0),\left(lkjhgfds, 0\right) \) and \( \left(lkjhgfds, asdfghjk\right) \) has area less than or equal to \( mnbvcxza \). Thus \( \frac{1}{2} asdfghjk\, lkjhgfds \leqq zxcvbnml \) or
\[
lkjhgfds \leqq \frac{2 zxcvbnml}{asdfghjk} .
\]

Equality is possible and gives the maximum value of \( lkjhgfds \) (for given \( zxcvbnml \) and \( asdfghjk \) ) when the graph of \( qzxwvtnp(mbvcxzpo) \) is the straight line \( qzxwvtnp(mbvcxzpo)=\left(asdfghjk / lkjhgfds\right) mbvcxzpo=\left(asdfghjk^{2} / 2 zxcvbnml\right) mbvcxzpo \).

Comment: If \( asdfghjk \) is zero or negative, there is no maximum time \( lkjhgfds \) for the traverse. In the case \( asdfghjk=0 \) the equation of motion
\[
poiuytre=zxcvbnml\left[3\left(mbvcxzpo / lkjhgfds\right)^{2}-3\left(mbvcxzpo / lkjhgfds\right)^{3}\right], \quad 0 \leqq mbvcxzpo \leqq lkjhgfds
\]
satisfies the conditions of the problem for any \( lkjhgfds>0 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$.  
For every coordinate $k=1,\dots ,m$ six positive real numbers  

\[
u_k,\;A_k,\;B_k,\;V_k,\;D_k ,\qquad 0<B_k<A_k ,
\]

are prescribed.  
They are called, respectively, initial velocity, upper and lower acceleration
bounds, required velocity gain and required extra displacement.

For $t\ge 0$ we call an $m$-tuple  
$v=(v_1,\dots ,v_m)$ of piece-wise $C^{1}$ functions an admissible {\bf
velocity profile} if for every coordinate $k$

\[
\dot x_k(t)=v_k(t),\qquad a_k(t)=\dot v_k(t)
\]

satisfies  

(R1) $a_k:[0,\infty)\to\mathbb R$ is bounded, measurable and
non-increasing;  

(R2) $A_k\ge a_k(t)\ge B_k$ for all $t\ge 0$ and $a_k(0)=A_k$  
(the value at $0$ may differ from $a_k(0+)$, so downward jumps at $t=0$ are
allowed).

The profile meets the prescribed terminal conditions at the instant $T>0$ when  

\[
v_k(T)=u_k+V_k,\qquad x_k(T)=u_k T+D_k\qquad (k=1,\dots ,m). \tag{$\star$}
\]

1.  Give, solely in terms of the data $\{A_k,B_k,V_k,D_k\}$,  

   (a) necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of at least one
       admissible profile verifying $(\star)$ at some time $T>0$;  

   (b) the complete set of times $T$ for which such a profile exists
       simultaneously in every coordinate.

2.  Whenever an admissible profile exists in all coordinates, determine  

\[
T_{\max}= \max\{T>0\;:\; (\star)\text{ can hold for every }k\},
\]

prove that this extremal time is unique, and construct an admissible profile
which attains it.

3.  Prove that no admissible profile can satisfy $(\star)$ at any time
$T>T_{\max}$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we analyse one coordinate, suppress the index $k$ and finally
synchronise the $m$ coordinates.

Notation for one coordinate  

\[
u,\;A,\;B,\;V,\;D,\qquad 0<B<A,\qquad
\Delta=A-B,\qquad v(0)=u .
\]

By translating the position variable we may assume $u=0$.

I.  One-coordinate analysis  
-------------------------------------------------

I.1  Integral identities  
For every admissible acceleration $a(t)$

\[
v(t)=\int_{0}^{t} a(s)\,ds,\qquad 
x(t)=\int_{0}^{t}(t-s)\,a(s)\,ds. \tag{1}
\]

Consequently the terminal requirements at some $T>0$ are

\[
\int_{0}^{T} a(s)\,ds=V,\qquad 
\int_{0}^{T}(T-s)a(s)\,ds=D. \tag{2}
\]

I.2  Two extremal envelopes  

Lower envelope.
Rearrangement (Chebyshev) gives for every admissible $a$

\[
x(T)=\int_{0}^{T}(T-s)a(s)\,ds
\;\ge\;\frac{1}{T}\Bigl(\int_{0}^{T}(T-s)\,ds\Bigr)
        \Bigl(\int_{0}^{T} a(s)\,ds\Bigr)
      =\frac{V\,T}{2}=:\,L(T). \tag{3}
\]

The value $L(T)$ is an {\em infimum}.  
It is attained exactly when $a(0)=A$ and $a(t)\equiv V/T$ for $t>0$.
This function is admissible because it is non-increasing,
$B\le V/T\le A$ (see (5) below) and satisfies the prescribed jump at $t=0$.

Upper envelope.  
Because the weight $T-s$ is strictly decreasing,  
$\Phi_T(a):=\int_{0}^{T}(T-s)a(s)\,ds$ is maximised, for fixed $V$,
by moving as much acceleration as early as possible.  
The optimal bang-bang profile is

\[
a(t)=\begin{cases}
A,& 0<t<\tau,\\
B,& \tau<t<T,
\end{cases}
\qquad 
\tau=\dfrac{V-BT}{\Delta}. \tag{4}
\]

Admissibility of (4) requires $0\le \tau\le T$, namely

\[
\frac{V}{A}\le T\le\frac{V}{B}. \tag{5}
\]

With (4) one obtains

\[
\begin{aligned}
U(T)&:=\max_a x(T)=
A\int_{0}^{\tau}(T-s)\,ds+
B\int_{\tau}^{T}(T-s)\,ds\\
&=V\,T-\frac{B\,T^{2}}{2}-\frac{(V-BT)^{2}}{2\Delta},\qquad
\frac{V}{A}\le T\le \frac{V}{B}. \tag{6}
\end{aligned}
\]

$U$ is strictly increasing and strictly concave on $(V/A,V/B)$; in particular  

\[
U\!\Bigl(\tfrac{V}{A}\Bigr)=\frac{V^{2}}{2A},\qquad 
U\!\Bigl(\tfrac{V}{B}\Bigr)=\frac{V^{2}}{2B}. \tag{7}
\]

I.3  Feasible times and existence criterion  

Combining (3), (5) and (6) we have

\[
T\text{ feasible }\Longleftrightarrow
\frac{V}{A}\le T\le\frac{V}{B}\;\hbox{ and }\;
L(T)\le D\le U(T). \tag{8}
\]

Because $L$ is linear and $U$ is concave, the set

\[
S=\{T>0\;:\;L(T)\le D\le U(T)\}
\]

is an interval $[T_{*},T_{\max}]$ or empty.
Writing $L(T)\le D$ gives $T\le 2D/V$, hence

\[
T_{\max}=\frac{2D}{V}. \tag{9}
\]

The lower endpoint $T_{*}$ is the smaller root of $U(T)=D$ inside the band
$[V/A,V/B]$:

\[
A B\,T^{2}-2A V\,T+V^{2}+2\Delta D=0. \tag{10}
\]

Its discriminant equals  
$\Delta_T=4A\Delta\,(V^{2}-2B D)$, therefore a real root exists iff

\[
2B D\le V^{2}\le 2A D. \tag{11}
\]

When (11) holds the smaller root is

\[
T_{*}=\frac{A V-\sqrt{A\Delta\,(V^{2}-2B D)}}{A B}. \tag{12}
\]

Proposition 1 (single coordinate).  
An admissible profile exists iff (11) holds.  
If so, every feasible time lies in  

\[
I=[T_{*},T_{\max}]
=\Bigl[\tfrac{A V-\sqrt{A\Delta\,(V^{2}-2B D)}}{A B},\;
       \tfrac{2D}{V}\Bigr]. \tag{13}
\]

I.4  Construction for an arbitrary feasible time  

Fix $T\in I$.
Define  

\[
\tau_{\max}=\frac{V-BT}{\Delta}\quad(\ge 0).
\]

For any $\tau\in[0,\tau_{\max}]$ put  

\[
c(\tau)=\frac{V-A\tau}{T-\tau}\qquad (B\le c(\tau)\le A),
\]

and choose the two-level acceleration

\[
a(0)=A,\qquad
a(t)=\begin{cases}
A,&0<t<\tau,\\
c(\tau),&\tau\le t\le T.
\end{cases} \tag{14}
\]

The integral constraint gives $\int_{0}^{T}a=V$ by construction.
A direct calculation shows

\[
x(T;\tau)=A\Bigl(T\tau-\frac{\tau^{2}}{2}\Bigr)
          +c(\tau)\,\frac{(T-\tau)^{2}}{2}. \tag{15}
\]

The map $\tau\mapsto x(T;\tau)$  
is continuous, $x(T;0)=L(T)$ and $x(T;\tau_{\max})=U(T)$.
Hence for every $D\in[L(T),U(T)]$
there exists a (unique) $\tau\in[0,\tau_{\max}]$ with $x(T;\tau)=D$.
Thus an admissible profile exists for every feasible $T$,
completing the proof that $I$ is indeed the full feasible-time set.

II.  Synchronising the $m$ coordinates  
-------------------------------------------------

For every $k$ put  

\[
\Delta_k=A_k-B_k,\qquad
T^{(k)}_{*},T^{(k)}_{\max}\text{ as in (12),(9)}. \tag{16}
\]

Coordinate $k$ is feasible iff

\[
2B_k D_k\le V_k^{2}\le 2A_k D_k\quad\text{(condition P$1_k$)}, \tag{17}
\]
and then it admits every time in the interval $I_k=[T^{(k)}_{*},T^{(k)}_{\max}]$.

The vector motion is feasible at time $T$ iff $T\in\bigcap_{k=1}^{m}I_k$.
Consequently

\[
T_{\max}:=\min_{1\le k\le m}T^{(k)}_{\max} \tag{18}
\]

is the largest {\em candidate} time, and simultaneous feasibility is equivalent
to  

\[
\text{(P$1_k$) for all }k,\qquad 
\max_{1\le k\le m}T^{(k)}_{*}\;\le\;
\min_{1\le k\le m}T^{(k)}_{\max}. \tag{19}
\]

III.  Construction of an admissible profile at $T_{\max}$  
-------------------------------------------------

Assume (19) holds and fix $T_{\max}$ given by (18).
For each coordinate $k$ choose

\[
\tau_k\in[0,\;\tau_{\max,k}],\qquad
\tau_{\max,k}=\frac{V_k-B_k T_{\max}}{\Delta_k},
\]

such that $x_k(T_{\max};\tau_k)=D_k$ where $x_k(\cdot;\tau)$ is the map in
(15).  
The existence and uniqueness of $\tau_k$ follow from the discussion in I.4.
Define  

\[
a_k(0)=A_k,\qquad
a_k(t)=\begin{cases}
A_k,&0<t<\tau_k,\\
c_k:=\dfrac{V_k-A_k\tau_k}{T_{\max}-\tau_k},&\tau_k\le t\le T_{\max},\\
B_k,&t>T_{\max}.
\end{cases}
\]

Because $B_k\le c_k\le A_k$, the function $a_k$ is admissible,
$v_k(T_{\max})=V_k$ and $x_k(T_{\max})=D_k$.  
Thus the $m$-tuple $(a_1,\dots ,a_m)$ furnishes an admissible
vector profile achieving all terminal conditions at the common
time $T_{\max}$.

IV.  Optimality of $T_{\max}$  
-------------------------------------------------

Let $T>T_{\max}$ and choose an index $k_0$ with
$T_{\max}=T^{(k_0)}_{\max}=2D_{k_0}/V_{k_0}$  
(the minimum in (18) is attained).
Then

\[
L_{k_0}(T)=\frac{V_{k_0}T}{2}\;>\;
\frac{V_{k_0}T^{(k_0)}_{\max}}{2}=D_{k_0}. \tag{20}
\]

By (3) the displacement $D_{k_0}$ cannot be produced at the later
time $T$, so no admissible vector profile can satisfy $(\star)$.
Therefore the value (18) is indeed the unique largest feasible time.

This completes the solution of items 1-3.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher structural complexity  
   • The motion is now in ℝᵐ with coordinate-wise but different upper and lower acceleration bounds (A_k and B_k).  
   • The accelerations are required to be monotone *and* locked between two distinct positive levels, introducing interacting upper and lower constraints.  
   • Both the first and the second integral moments of a(t) are prescribed (velocity and displacement), producing a genuine two-moment optimisation problem.

2. Deeper theory  
   • The solution needs a functional-analytic extreme-point argument (the bang–bang principle) on an infinite-dimensional convex set of admissible accelerations.  
   • A rearrangement / Chebyshev inequality is invoked to justify that extremals concentrate the larger acceleration values near t=0.  
   • The resulting optimisation reduces to manipulating coupled nonlinear equations, leading to a quadratic whose discriminant itself contains the data in a non-trivial way.

3. Additional constraints and interacting concepts  
   • The presence of both an upper and a lower bound on acceleration forces the extremal profile to have *two* distinct constant levels rather than the single-level solution of the original problem.  
   • Feasibility now demands the non-obvious condition 2A_k D_k ≥ V_k²; recognising and proving this necessity is an extra step absent from the original.  
   • Finally, the global answer is the *minimum* of m individual maxima, coupling m one-dimensional optimisation subproblems into a single multi-constraint extremal problem.

All these layers make the enhanced problem substantially harder: it demands variational thinking, functional inequalities, piece-wise optimisation, and the handling of non-trivial algebraic conditions—well beyond the elementary area‐under-curve argument of the original.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$.  For every coordinate $k=1,\dots ,m$ six positive parameters are prescribed  

\[
u_k,\;A_k,\;B_k,\;V_k,\;D_k\qquad (0<B_k<A_k),
\]

called, respectively, initial velocity, initial upper acceleration bound, lower acceleration bound, required velocity gain, and required extra displacement.  

For $t\ge 0$ we call an $m$-tuple $v=(v_1,\dots ,v_m)$ of piece-wise $C^{1}$ (hence absolutely continuous) functions an admissible velocity profile if, for every coordinate $k$,

\[
\dot x_k(t)=v_k(t),\qquad a_k(t)=\dot v_k(t),
\]

satisfies  

(R1) each $a_k$ is bounded, measurable and non-increasing on $[0,\infty)$;  

(R2) $A_k\ge a_k(t)\ge B_k$ for all $t\ge 0$ and $a_k(0)=A_k$.  
(The value $a_k(0)$ may differ from $a_k(0+)$, so instantaneous downward jumps are allowed.)

The profile meets the prescribed terminal conditions at the instant $T>0$ when  

\[
v_k(T)=u_k+V_k,\qquad x_k(T)=u_k\,T+D_k\qquad(k=1,\dots ,m). \tag{$\star$}
\]

1.  Express, solely in terms of the data $\{A_k,B_k,V_k,D_k\}$,  

   (a) the necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of at least one admissible profile verifying $(\star)$ at some time $T>0$;  

   (b) the complete set of times $T$ for which such a profile exists in every single coordinate.

2.  When an admissible profile exists in all coordinates, determine  

\[
T_{\max}=\hbox{the (unique) largest time for which }(\star)\hbox{ can hold},
\]

and construct an admissible profile that realises $T_{\max}$.

3.  Prove that no admissible profile can satisfy $(\star)$ at any time $T>T_{\max}$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write all formulas for a single coordinate and finally synchronise the $m$ coordinates.

Notation for one coordinate.  
Fix $k$ and suppress the index:

\[
u=u_k,\;A=A_k,\;B=B_k,\;V=V_k,\;D=D_k,\;\Delta=A-B>0,\;v(0)=u.
\]

By translating the position variable we may assume $u=0$.

I.  One-coordinate analysis  
-------------------------------------------------

I.1  Integral identities  
For every admissible acceleration $a(t)$

\[
v(t)=\int_{0}^{t} a(s)\,ds,\qquad 
x(t)=\int_{0}^{t}(t-s)a(s)\,ds. \tag{1}
\]

Hence the terminal requirements at some $T>0$ read

\[
\int_{0}^{T} a(s)\,ds=V,\qquad 
\int_{0}^{T}(T-s)a(s)\,ds=D. \tag{2}
\]

Define

\[
L(T):=\frac{V\,T}{2}\quad\text{(minimal displacement at time $T$)},\qquad 
U(T):=\max_{a\text{ admissible, (2) holds}} x(T).\tag{3}
\]

I.2  The upper envelope $U(T)$  
Because $(T-s)$ is strictly decreasing in $s$, the functional
$\Phi_T(a)=\int_{0}^{T}(T-s)\,a(s)\,ds$ is maximised, for fixed $V$, by placing as much
acceleration as possible near the origin.  
The optimal (bang-bang) profile is  

\[
a(t)=\begin{cases}
A,& 0\le t\le \tau,\\[2pt]
B,& \tau\le t\le T,
\end{cases}\qquad 
\tau=\dfrac{V-BT}{\Delta}. \tag{4}
\]

Admissibility of (4) requires $0\le\tau\le T$, i.e.

\[
\frac{V}{A}\le T\le\frac{V}{B}. \tag{5}
\]

With (4) one obtains

\[
\begin{aligned}
x(T)&=\Delta T\tau+\frac{B\,T^{2}}{2}-\frac{\Delta \tau^{2}}{2} \\
&=V\,T-\frac{B\,T^{2}}{2}-\frac{(V-BT)^{2}}{2\Delta}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus  

\[
U(T)=V\,T-\frac{B\,T^{2}}{2}-\frac{(V-BT)^{2}}{2\Delta},\qquad \frac{V}{A}\le T\le\frac{V}{B}. \tag{6}
\]

$U$ is strictly increasing and strictly concave on $(V/A,V/B)$; in particular  

\[
U\!\Bigl(\frac{V}{A}\Bigr)=\frac{V^{2}}{2A},\qquad 
U\!\Bigl(\frac{V}{B}\Bigr)=\frac{V^{2}}{2B}. \tag{7}
\]

I.3  The lower envelope $L(T)$  
Chebyshev's inequality (or any rearrangement argument) applied to the decreasing
sequence $T-s$ and the non-increasing $a(s)$ gives

\[
x(T)=\int_{0}^{T}(T-s)a(s)\,ds\;\ge\;\frac{V\,T}{2}=L(T), \tag{8}
\]

and the inequality is sharp (take $a(t)\equiv V/T$).  

I.4  Feasible times for one coordinate  

Combining (5), (6) and (8) we have  

\[
T\text{ feasible}\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\frac{V}{A}\le T\le\frac{V}{B},\quad 
L(T)\le D\le U(T). \tag{9}
\]

Because $L$ is linear increasing and $U$ is strictly concave increasing,
the feasible-time set is either empty or an interval $[T_{*},T_{\max}]$.

Upper end-point.  
The inequality $L(T)\le D$ forces $T\le 2D/V$, hence

\[
T_{\max}=\frac{2D}{V}. \tag{10}
\]

Lower end-point.  
$T_{*}$ is the smaller root (inside the interval (5)) of $U(T)=D$:

\[
A B\,T^{2}-2A V\,T+V^{2}+2\Delta D=0. \tag{11}
\]

Writing the discriminant $\Delta_T$ gives

\[
\Delta_T=4A\Delta\,(V^{2}-2B D),
\]

so a real root exists iff  

\[
V^{2}\ge 2B D. \tag{12}
\]

The smaller root is  

\[
T_{*}=\frac{A V-\sqrt{A\Delta\,(V^{2}-2B D)}}{A B}. \tag{13}
\]

Existence criterion.  
Reality of (13) and inclusion $T_{*}\ge V/A$ together are equivalent to  

\[
2B D\le V^{2}\le 2A D. \tag{14}
\]

Therefore:

Proposition (one coordinate).  
An admissible profile exists iff (14) holds.  
If so, every feasible time lies in the interval  

\[
I=[T_{*},T_{\max}]
=\Bigl[\frac{A V-\sqrt{A\Delta\,(V^{2}-2B D)}}{A B},\;
\frac{2D}{V}\Bigr]. \tag{15}
\]

II.  Synchronising the $m$ coordinates  
-------------------------------------------------

For every $k$ put  

\[
\Delta_k=A_k-B_k,\quad 
T^{(k)}_{*}=\frac{A_k V_k-\sqrt{A_k\Delta_k\,(V_k^{2}-2B_k D_k)}}{A_k B_k},\quad
T^{(k)}_{\max}=\frac{2D_k}{V_k}. \tag{16}
\]

Coordinate $k$ is feasible iff  

\[
2B_k D_k\le V_k^{2}\le 2A_k D_k, \tag{P1$_k$}
\]
and then it admits every time in $I_k=[T^{(k)}_{*},T^{(k)}_{\max}]$.

The vector motion is feasible at time $T$ iff $T\in\bigcap_{k=1}^{m}I_k$.  
Consequently

\[
T_{\max}:=\min_{1\le k\le m}T^{(k)}_{\max} \tag{17}
\]

is the largest candidate time, and simultaneous feasibility is equivalent to  

\[
\text{(P1$_k$) for every }k,\qquad 
\max_{1\le k\le m}T^{(k)}_{*}\le\min_{1\le k\le m}T^{(k)}_{\max}. \tag{18}
\]

III.  Construction of an admissible profile at $T_{\max}$  
-------------------------------------------------

Fix $T_{\max}$ given by (17) and assume (18) holds.  
For each coordinate $k$ exactly one of the following mutually exclusive
situations occurs.

Case A: $T_{\max}<T^{(k)}_{\max}$.  
Then $T_{\max}\ge T^{(k)}_{*}$ and (4) gives a non-negative switch time  

\[
\tau_k=\frac{V_k-B_k T_{\max}}{A_k-B_k}\in[0,T_{\max}].
\]

Choose the bang-bang profile (4) with this $\tau_k$; it yields
$v_k(T_{\max})=V_k,\;x_k(T_{\max})=D_k$.

Case B: $T_{\max}=T^{(k)}_{\max}=2D_k/V_k$ and $V_k/B_k>T_{\max}$.  
Then the constant value $a_k=V_k/T_{\max}$ satisfies $B_k\le a_k\le A_k$.  
Set  

\[
a_k(0)=A_k,\qquad a_k(t)=\frac{V_k}{T_{\max}}\quad(0<t\le T_{\max}). \tag{19}
\]

Case C (limit case): $T_{\max}=V_k/B_k=2D_k/V_k$ (equivalently $V_k^{2}=2B_k D_k$).  
Take $a_k(t)\equiv B_k\;(t>0)$.  
Again $v_k(T_{\max})=V_k,\;x_k(T_{\max})=D_k$.

Combining the $m$ coordinates furnishes an admissible vector
profile satisfying all terminal conditions at the common time $T_{\max}$.

IV.  Optimality of $T_{\max}$  
-------------------------------------------------

Let $T>T_{\max}$ and choose a coordinate $k_{0}$ with
$T_{\max}=T^{(k_0)}_{\max}=2D_{k_0}/V_{k_0}$.
Then $T>T^{(k_0)}_{\max}$, so by (8)

\[
L_{k_0}(T)=\frac{V_{k_0}T}{2}>\frac{V_{k_0}T^{(k_0)}_{\max}}{2}=D_{k_0}. \tag{20}
\]

Consequently, for coordinate $k_{0}$ no admissible acceleration can produce the
required displacement $D_{k_0}$ at the later time $T$.  Hence no admissible
vector profile can satisfy $(\star)$ for any $T>T_{\max}$; the value
(17) is therefore maximal.

This completes the proof.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher structural complexity  
   • The motion is now in ℝᵐ with coordinate-wise but different upper and lower acceleration bounds (A_k and B_k).  
   • The accelerations are required to be monotone *and* locked between two distinct positive levels, introducing interacting upper and lower constraints.  
   • Both the first and the second integral moments of a(t) are prescribed (velocity and displacement), producing a genuine two-moment optimisation problem.

2. Deeper theory  
   • The solution needs a functional-analytic extreme-point argument (the bang–bang principle) on an infinite-dimensional convex set of admissible accelerations.  
   • A rearrangement / Chebyshev inequality is invoked to justify that extremals concentrate the larger acceleration values near t=0.  
   • The resulting optimisation reduces to manipulating coupled nonlinear equations, leading to a quadratic whose discriminant itself contains the data in a non-trivial way.

3. Additional constraints and interacting concepts  
   • The presence of both an upper and a lower bound on acceleration forces the extremal profile to have *two* distinct constant levels rather than the single-level solution of the original problem.  
   • Feasibility now demands the non-obvious condition 2A_k D_k ≥ V_k²; recognising and proving this necessity is an extra step absent from the original.  
   • Finally, the global answer is the *minimum* of m individual maxima, coupling m one-dimensional optimisation subproblems into a single multi-constraint extremal problem.

All these layers make the enhanced problem substantially harder: it demands variational thinking, functional inequalities, piece-wise optimisation, and the handling of non-trivial algebraic conditions—well beyond the elementary area‐under-curve argument of the original.
